Patch Set 2:

I have a general question here - Are we running with preempt enabled somewhere?  Nearly all traditional 'computer' installs of Linux run with preempt disabled, it's basically just real time and maybe a few other things that run with preempt enabled.  Or that was true a few years ago, at least...

Is this a bug you've seen, or just something you're concerned about?

The change seems reasonable, just wondering - I think if we're really going to support preempt enabled kernels there's more to do.  (other per CPU stuff to address, basically)

I thought that one case that preempt could happen is if one process use its
timeslice?

Patch Set 2:

(1 comment)

Patch Set 2:

About timeslice based preemption:
This is true for userspace, but not the kernel, unless you've got a special config option (I think it's PREEMPT_RT?).  Without preempt enabled, kernel processes cannot be preempted (interrupts are a special case, but they still run on the same CPU, they just interrupt the current process - not switch to another).  Kernel processes can only switch CPUs if they choose to go to sleep.

Consider cond_resched() - It's a process saying "I can be scheduled if needed".  That's specifically necessary because they would not be scheduled if they did not *volunteer* to be scheduled.

This is also why sleeping holding a spinlock isn't safe (in kernel).   Your process sleeps, and then if every CPU on the system tries to get that spinlock, your process will never be woken up - because those processes keep spinning forever.
Uploaded patch set 3.

> About timeslice based preemption:
 > This is true for userspace, but not the kernel, unless you've got a
 > special config option (I think it's PREEMPT_RT?).  Without preempt
 > enabled, kernel processes cannot be preempted (interrupts are a
 > special case, but they still run on the same CPU, they just
 > interrupt the current process - not switch to another).  Kernel
 > processes can only switch CPUs if they choose to go to sleep.
 > 
 > Consider cond_resched() - It's a process saying "I can be scheduled
 > if needed".  That's specifically necessary because they would not
 > be scheduled if they did not *volunteer* to be scheduled.
 > 
 > This is also why sleeping holding a spinlock isn't safe (in
 > kernel).   Your process sleeps, and then if every CPU on the system
 > tries to get that spinlock, your process will never be woken up -
 > because those processes keep spinning forever.

Thanks for teaching this information Patrick.
So it seems the problem could only happen with some config enabled.
I am not sure whether this could really happen in real use case.

But from a code developer perspectively, better fix it.
